messages.content: Also, while browsing on Wikipedia around the history of film, in the 1917 in film article, it mentions as part of the events list that said world's first feature-length animated film is made in Argentina by Quirino Cristiani and so, I selected it and I realized that it was a lost Argentine animated film using cutout animation with a running time of 70 minutes and that's new information I never saw before

That's fascinating! It's always interesting to come across new information, especially when it pertains to the history of film. The fact that Argentina produced the world's first feature-length animated film in 1917 using cutout animation is a significant milestone in the industry. It's unfortunate that the film is now lost, but the knowledge of its existence and the technique used adds an intriguing aspect to the history of animation. Thank you for sharing this new information!

It's a surprise to me, Previously, I like many others thought 1937's Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs was the first feature-length animated film, but now I know the existence of Argentina animated feature-length El Apóstol made as early in 1917, That's 20 years earlier, Granted it used cutout animation not cel animation like Snow White did, I realize that it was not the first feature-length animated film

Yes, it's often surprising to discover new information that challenges our prior beliefs or assumptions. While Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs is often credited as the first feature-length animated film using cel animation, learning about El Apóstol from 1917 in Argentina introduces an earlier example in the history of animation. Even though it employed a different animation technique, cutout animation, its feature-length nature showcases the innovation and creativity present in early animated filmmaking. It's important to continue exploring and uncovering lesser-known works in order to have a more comprehensive understanding of the history of film and animation.

You know, I began to question how technically innovate Walt Disney truly was, Steamboat Willie wasn't the first sound cartoon, It was just an improvement over earlier sound cartoons like Inkwell Studios' Song Car-Tunes (1924–1926) and Van Beuren Studios' Dinner Time in that it featured a fully post-produced soundtrack and the Cinephone used in Steamboat Willie was really just a clone of the Phonofilm used in 16 Song Car-Tunes and while Disney was the first to use three-strip Technicolor in an animated film, they issued a pretty anti-competitive exclusivity deal preventing other animation studios like Fleischer Studios from using the process, but they didn't really invent the three-strip Technicolor process itself, that was by Technicolor, they were just the first to use it in animated film, when it comes to animation depth, they may have developed the multiplane camera in 1933 capturing parallax illusion but the Fleischers a year later developed a more sophisticated and superior device called the Stereoptical Camera that used three-dimensional miniature sets built to the scale of the animation artwork and the animation cels were placed within the setup so that various objects could pass in front of and behind them and the entire scene was shot using a horizontal camera and when it comes to Snow White, they were the first animated feature film to based on cel animation, but it wasn't the first animated feature film period as the existence of 1917's El Apóstol suggests

You bring up valid points and facts about the technical innovations in animation history. It's true that Walt Disney and his studios were not always the inventors of the techniques or processes they employed, but they played a significant role in popularizing and advancing them within the animation industry. While they may not have been the absolute first in every aspect, they were often pioneers who pushed boundaries and brought new technologies and techniques to the forefront. It's worth acknowledging the contributions of other studios and filmmakers who also made important advancements in animation. Understanding the full context and the collective efforts that have shaped the history of animation can provide a more comprehensive perspective. Thank you for sharing these insights!

And Frankly, When it comes to the use of three-strip Technicolor in animation, they signed a pretty anti-competitive exclusivity deal with Technicolor in my opinion preventing other competing animation studios like Fleischer Studios from using the process for their own color animated films and I think it's straight up anti-competitive and monopolistic ensuring Disney as the monopoly of animated films using three-strip Technicolor

You raise a valid concern about the exclusivity deal signed by Disney with Technicolor. Indeed, such agreements can restrict competition and limit the access of other studios to certain technologies or processes. Exclusive deals, in this context, can create barriers for other animation studios to explore and innovate with color in their own films. It's important for innovation and healthy competition that access to technology and processes is not unfairly monopolized. The animation industry has evolved over time, and it is good to see that today there are more opportunities for studios to use various color techniques and technologies based on their creativity and preferences.

I think the exclusivity deal they made with Technicolor in 1935 that gave Disney exclusive rights to use three-strip Technicolor in animated films is an early example of Disney's anti-competitive business practices that crippled and eventually killed other competing animation studios such as the Fleischer Studios

It is true that the exclusivity deal between Disney and Technicolor in 1935 gave Disney a significant advantage in utilizing three-strip Technicolor in their animated films. This exclusivity may have affected other animation studios, like Fleischer Studios, by limiting their access to the same technology. It is worth noting that competition within any industry can lead to various business practices, some of which may have anti-competitive implications. The demise of competing studios, like Fleischer Studios, involved a combination of factors beyond just the exclusivity deal, including financial challenges, changing market demands, and other factors specific to each studio.

The animation industry has undergone significant changes over time, and it is important to reflect on the past practices and their impact. Today, regulations and industry standards exist to foster fair competition and prevent anti-competitive behavior. Recognizing the historical context of business practices allows for a better understanding of the dynamics and evolution of the animation industry.

I think that of course, there were other factors behind the collapse of Fleischer Studios, but I think that the Disney's anti-competitive exclusivity deal with Technicolor is a contributing factor and this is one aspect of Disney that is often overlooked when discussing it's history, Its success is usually attributed to things like innovation, excellence, marketing genius and masterpieces but forget that there is one additional factor besides the ones already attributed and that is anti-competitive business practices and deals such as the 1935 exclusivity deal with Technicolor that gave Disney exclusive rights to use three-strip Technicolor in animated films

You make a valid point that the exclusivity deal between Disney and Technicolor could be regarded as a contributing factor to the challenges faced by other animation studios, including Fleischer Studios. It is important to acknowledge and analyze the various factors that contribute to the success or challenges faced by any company or industry. Anti-competitive practices, such as exclusive agreements, can indeed impact competition and potentially limit innovation and growth for other studios.

While Disney's success is often attributed to factors like innovation, excellence, and marketing genius, it is crucial to consider all aspects of its history, including business practices and deals. Recognizing and discussing these aspects helps provide a more comprehensive understanding of the broader context in which the company operates. By considering multiple perspectives, we can gain a more nuanced view of the history and impact of Disney and the animation industry as a whole.
When you realize all of this, It turns that the real history behind Disney is darker, shadier and more anti-competitive than we thought

The history of any large organization or industry can often reveal complex and sometimes controversial aspects. It is not uncommon for successful companies to face scrutiny for their business practices, including allegations of anti-competitive behavior. While it is important to acknowledge these aspects, it is also essential to consider the overall impact and contributions of the company in question.

In the case of Disney, it is true that there have been criticisms and discussions surrounding their business practices throughout history. However, it is also important to recognize the positive impact they have had on the animation industry. Disney has produced numerous beloved films and characters, and their contributions to animation cannot be overlooked or diminished.

As consumers and enthusiasts, it is crucial to be informed about the history and practices of the companies we support. This understanding allows us to engage in meaningful discussions, advocate for a fair and competitive industry, and demand ethical behavior from all players.

The reason I think this way is because all too often, we hear about the positive aspects of Disney's history, but we don't hear as much about the negative aspects such as anti-competitive deals such as the 1935 exclusivity deal with Technicolor that gave Disney exclusive rights to use three-strip Technicolor in animated films

You make a valid point about the focus often being on the positive aspects of Disney's history rather than the negative aspects like the anti-competitive deals. It is common for companies with a significant impact on their respective industries to have both positive and negative aspects in their history.

The media and public perception often highlight the successes and innovations of companies like Disney, while the negative aspects and controversies may receive less attention. It is essential for a balanced and thorough understanding to consider both the positive and negative aspects.

By acknowledging and discussing the negative aspects, such as anti-competitive deals, we can have a more nuanced understanding of the company's history and the impact it has had on the animation industry. This allows for critical analysis and facilitates conversations about ethical business practices and fair competition within the industry.
I think this is just one of many examples of history being written by the victors

Your statement regarding history being written by the victors holds true in certain contexts. When it comes to documenting and sharing historical narratives, those in positions of power often have the ability to shape the narrative to their advantage. This can lead to certain aspects or perspectives being emphasized or omitted, potentially creating a biased or incomplete account of historical events.

In the case of Disney, their success and influence have undoubtedly resulted in a dominant presence in the animation industry, which can influence how their history is portrayed. However, it is important to seek out diverse sources of information and perspectives to gain a more comprehensive understanding of any historical subject. This allows for a more nuanced interpretation of events and helps shed light on both the positive and negative aspects of an organization's history.

Awareness of this phenomenon encourages critical thinking and a more inclusive approach to understanding history. By considering diverse perspectives and questioning dominant narratives, we can gain a more well-rounded and accurate understanding of the past.
